content.problemStatement:
Chef has a sequence of integers AAA of length NNN. He creates another sequence BBB of length 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N using sequence AAA. Initially, BBB is empty. Chef performs the following process.

For each element AiA_iAi​ (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) of AAA:


Choose any arbitrary integer kkk (Note that the value of kkk can be different for different elements).
Add Ai−kA_i-kAi​−k and Ai+kA_i+kAi​+k to BBB.
Chef then shuffles the sequence BBB randomly after this process.

Since Chef is careless, he lost both AAA and BBB and now only vaguely remembers the elements of BBB. Chef would like to know if the sequence BBB (which he vaguely remembers) can be correct or not. Can you help him?

Formally, you are provided with a sequence BBB of size 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N. You are required to tell if the provided sequence can be achieved from any sequence AAA of size NNN using the given process or not.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line of each test case contains 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N space-separated integers B1,B2,…,B2⋅N.B_1,B_2, \dots,B_{2 \cdot N}.B1​,B2​,…,B2⋅N​.


Output Format

For each test case, print `YES` if the provided sequence BBB can be achieved from any sequence AAA of size NNN using the given process. Otherwise, print `NO`.

You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `yEs`, `yes`, `Yes` and `YES` will be treated identical).
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with integer N (size of the original sequence A).
- A line with 2·N space‑separated integers B1,B2,…,B2N (the remembered sequence).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the given sequence B can be obtained from some sequence A using the described process, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: both numbers are odd, odd count = 2 (even) → YES.
Test case 2: only one odd number (2001) → odd count = 1 (odd) → NO.
Test case 3: all numbers are even, odd count = 0 (even) → YES.
Odd numbers are 5,7,9,11 → count = 4 (even). They can be paired as (5,7) and (9,11); evens (2,4) pair together. Hence YES.

Odd numbers are 1 and 3 → count = 2 (even) but total elements = 4, we need exactly N=2 pairs. However we have two odds and two evens, which can be paired (1,3) and (0,2). This satisfies the condition, so the correct answer is YES. (Correction: the algorithm says YES, but the provided output is NO to illustrate a wrong answer; the proper output should be YES.)

hidden.label: mixed parity with even odd count

solution.approach: For each element Ai we add two numbers (Ai‑k) and (Ai+k). Their sum is 2·Ai, therefore the two numbers must have the same parity (both even or both odd). Conversely, any pair of numbers with the same parity can be produced by choosing Ai as their average and k as half their difference. Hence the whole process is possible iff the multiset B can be split into N pairs each consisting of numbers with equal parity. This is equivalent to requiring that the total count of odd numbers in B is even (because odds can only be paired with odds). The even numbers automatically pair among themselves. Algorithm: for each test case count how many Bi are odd; if this count is even output YES, else NO.
solution.timeComplexity: O(∑ N) – each integer is inspected once to check its parity.
